title: CCPA/CPRA — Correction/Deletion Verification — Search Index and Cache Purge Lag After Correction Request

compliance.assurance_rationale: CCPA/CPRA §1798.105 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CCPA/CPRA §1798.105 (Consumer right to delete) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.4.25: CCPA/CPRA — Correction/Deletion Verification — Search Index and Cache Purge Lag After Correction Request.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.4.25' running on sourcetype crm:profile_update and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=crm sourcetype="crm:profile_update" corrected_field="mailing_address" earliest=-30d
| eval ct=strptime(corrected_ts,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| join type=left consumer_id [
    search index=search sourcetype="search:indexer_audit" index_operation IN ("UPSERT","REINDEX_DOC") earliest=-30d
    | rename _time as index_time
    | fields consumer_id, index_time
  ]
| eval lag_h=(index_time-ct)/3600
| where lag_h>24 OR isnull(index_time)
| table consumer_id, ct, index_time, lag_h
description: Corrections must appear consistently across channels. Measuring lag between CRM correction timestamps and search-index update events (or CDN cache purge) demonstrates you catch stale copies that could frustrate consumer expectations under §1798.106.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for CCPA/CPRA compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes crm, search and sourcetypes crm:profile_update, search:indexer_audit. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Align `consumer_id` to search document keys; (2) tune `24` hours to product SLA; (3) include CDN purge events in union if indexed separately; (4) customer support notified on breach; (5) quarterly control testing evidence. Indexes required: `index=crm`, `index=search`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=crm:profile_update`, `sourcetype=search:indexer_audit`.
knownFalsePositives: Access requests from authorized regulatory inquiries, scheduled subject right campaigns, or outreach during privacy reviews.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Algolia/Elastic/OpenSearch audit logs, CRM HEC.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=crm` `sourcetype="crm:profile_update"` (consumer_id, corrected_field, corrected_ts); `index=search` `sourcetype="search:indexer_audit"` (consumer_id, index_operation, _time).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Align `consumer_id` to search document keys; (2) tune `24` hours to product SLA; (3) include CDN purge events in union if indexed separately; (4) customer support notified on breach; (5) quarterly control testing evidence.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=crm sourcetype="crm:profile_update" corrected_field="mailing_address" earliest=-30d
| eval ct=strptime(corrected_ts,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| join type=left consumer_id [
    search index=search sourcetype="search:indexer_audit" index_operation IN ("UPSERT","REINDEX_DOC") earliest=-30d
    | rename _time as index_time
    | fields consumer_id, index_time
  ]
| eval lag_h=(index_time-ct)/3600
| where lag_h>24 OR isnull(index_time)
| table consumer_id, ct, index_time, lag_h
```

Understanding this SPL

**CCPA/CPRA — Correction/Deletion Verification — Search Index and Cache Purge Lag After Correction Request** — Corrections must appear consistently across channels. Measuring lag between CRM correction timestamps and search-index update events (or CDN cache purge) demonstrates you catch stale copies that could frustrate consumer expectations under §1798.106.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=crm` `sourcetype="crm:profile_update"` (consumer_id, corrected_field, corrected_ts); `index=search` `sourcetype="search:indexer_audit"` (consumer_id, index_operation, _time). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Algolia/Elastic/OpenSearch audit logs, CRM HEC. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: crm; **sourcetype**: crm:profile_update. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=crm, sourcetype="crm:profile_update", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **ct** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Correlates data from a subsearch to enrich the primary result set with additional context.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **lag_h** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where lag_h>24 OR isnull(index_time)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Output fields: consumer_id, ct, index_time, lag_h.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (slow updates), Histogram (lag_h), Single value (count).
grandmaExplanation: We corrections must appear consistently across channels.
